# 4 best practices for building a solid ESG strategy

Are you puzzling together your ESG strategy? In this blog, we're covering our four best tips for creating a solid foundation of your strategy.

Follow this blog with our popular webinar *A smooth and stress-free sustainability reporting process:*

![Watch webinar](https://no-cache.hubspot.com/cta/default/2989273/eb055b7a-b697-4347-8496-c741052c2ff5.png)
![4 best practices for building a solid ESG strategy](https://blog.worldfavor.com/hubfs/Best%20practices%20for%20building%20your%20ESG%20strategy.png)

**What is an ESG strategy?**An ESG strategy is the process of disclosing data to transparently show how an organization performs across three key areas: environmental sustainability, social sustainability, and corporate governance.

Today, investors are using ESG as the screening method for choosing companies to invest in and understanding their portfolio companies. As a result, more and more companies are implementing their own ESG reporting strategy.

Environmental, social, and governance (ESG) factors are fast becoming key value drivers in investment decisions. In fact, [around 74% of the world’s largest investors are focusing more on ESG investing,](https://www.edie.net/cop27-primer-climate-finance/?utm_campaign=edie%20daily%20news%20alert&utm_source=AdestraCampaign&utm_medium=Email&utm+content=COP27%20Primer%3A%20Climate%20Finance) according to a recent edie report.

Organizations of all sizes are beginning to feel the pressure to start planning out their ESG reporting strategy. Developing an ESG strategy can feel overwhelming and challenging but with the right tools and practices organizations can build a strategy that that supports risk management and helps to drive positive impact. In this blog, we have gathered some of the best tips and tricks we have learned over the years to help you succeed with your ESG strategy and why it's important.[![On-demand webinar: Smooth sustainability reporting](https://no-cache.hubspot.com/cta/default/2989273/4f3ab5ed-ef0b-40ad-9b9f-526893240bb5.png)](https://cta-redirect.hubspot.com/cta/redirect/2989273/4f3ab5ed-ef0b-40ad-9b9f-526893240bb5)

![On-demand webinar: Smooth sustainability reporting](https://no-cache.hubspot.com/cta/default/2989273/4f3ab5ed-ef0b-40ad-9b9f-526893240bb5.png)

### **Why is ESG reporting important?**

We’re living in a climate emergency, and understanding environmental and social risks has become increasingly important for investment decisions.

ESG criteria have become an essential part of any risk management as not only does it help the organization to have a positive impact on the world, but reporting on ESG has shown to have a positive impact on the organization as well. For example:

### **Best practices for building an ESG strategy**

#### **1.** **Set up a dedicated ESG team**

#### **2. Align to suitable global & regulatory frameworks**

Once you got your dedicated ESG team in place, the next step is to find a suitable framework to align your reporting process. Using a specific framework will help guide the reporting process by showing companies where to look, what to measure and how to communicate their findings.

Finding the relevant reporting framework is actually more important than many may realize. The jungle of different frameworks and standards has opened the door to greenwashing, as companies can “cherry-pick” between the reporting systems that would be in their favor. Too often, organizations use their end goals as the starting point when deciding what to measure – which seems logical, right?: “This is what we want to achieve, so let’s measure whether we achieve it or not.” The essential purpose of ESG is to measure the organization’s overall sustainability performance – and to identify risks and opportunities you do not always see in the first place. Not aligning your ESG strategy with an established framework suited for your organization jeopardizes the quality of your data and, in the worst case, lets risky business slip through.

There is a great deal of different well-known and established ESG frameworks to align with, including:

[Don’t miss our reporting frameworks blog to learn more about each of these frameworks.](https://blog.worldfavor.com/5-sustainability-reporting-frameworks-to-help-your-organization-set-priorities)

#### [Smooth and Stress-Free Sustainability Reporting Process](https://cta-redirect.hubspot.com/cta/redirect/2989273/81783323-feba-444c-a4d7-923f0ff682ee)

![ Smooth and Stress-Free Sustainability Reporting Process](https://no-cache.hubspot.com/cta/default/2989273/81783323-feba-444c-a4d7-923f0ff682ee.png)

#### **3. Invest in an efficient software tool to collect and monitor ESG data from multiple sources**

The backbone of any successful ESG story is data. At the same time, data quality is also the biggest challenge organizations must overcome. This is especially true for large businesses and financial institutions with many subsidiaries, partners, and suppliers to gather data from. Achieving consistent and auditable ESG data is not an easy task, but that is what stakeholders, such as investors and customers, demand – and companies have no other option than to adapt and deliver. As a result, [more companies are beginning to see the benefits of investing in a sustainability software for collecting and analyzing their big data.](/5-measurable-ways-to-benefit-from-a-sustainability-software) It helps a company to create clarity and insight on a much broader and deeper level than possible without one and eliminate human errors and miscalculations that could potentially end up as nonintentional greenwashing.

#### **4. Be transparent with your process**

Transparency is a crucial component of your ESG strategy, as, without it, your data lacks accuracy, validity, and timeliness. Build credibility and trust by communicating with your stakeholders about where you are in your process – both your wins and losses. Stakeholders today do not want impressive-sounding targets – they want the facts.

# 9 best practices for ESG reporting & strategy

![An ESG team discussing ESG best practices](/_next/image?url=https%3A%2F%2Fcdn.sanity.io%2Fimages%2F33u1mixi%2Fproduction%2Fa741d8dc206eda89801b3b89b0cceda7c9838b38-1080x720.jpg&w=2048&q=75)

[Environmental, social and governance (ESG)](https://www.diligent.com/resources/guides/esg) issues are top-of-mind for many corporations. In fact, a recent survey showed that [43% of shareholders](https://www.nysscpa.org/news/publications/the-trusted-professional/article/survey-majority-of-corporate-directors-see-esg-reporting-as-essential-many-take-steps-toward-board-diversity) want to talk to boards about ESG — more than any other topic on the board agenda. As a result, many corporations are scrambling to identify and implement ESG best practices: the set of policies and protocols that make up an effective ESG program.

At the same time, corporate boards are becoming more [willing to disclose](https://www.wsj.com/articles/corporate-boards-are-disclosing-more-on-diversity-of-directors-esg-insight-d6c1f17a) ESG-related information, like board diversity. This signals a focus on ESG reporting practices as a key way corporations will double down on their efforts now and in the future. To help you keep up, this article will explain:

## Why is effective ESG reporting important?

[ESG reporting](https://www.diligent.com/resources/blog/esg-reporting) is essential to risk management. Corporations face more climate risk than ever, and consumers worldwide are holding companies to a higher standard, causing boards to look to ESG as a way to adapt to this new normal. Many institutions and investors have also [prioritized ESG](https://www.diligent.com/resources/blog/why-is-esg-important) in recent years, giving corporations a powerful [financial motivation](https://www.diligent.com/resources/blog/sustainable-finance) to commit to ESG.

When organizations adopt clear ESG reporting and best practices, they:

## Our 9 ESG best practices

### 1. Assemble an ESG team

ESG is complex, and it’s more than implementing a recycling program or using compostable packing for your products. So, while it may be tempting to have team members double duty as ESG advocates alongside their current roles, it’s a critical ESG best practice to create a dedicated team.

This can include a sustainability consultant, a diversity consultant, a risk manager dedicated to ESG risks and more. Be sure to prioritize [diversity](https://www.diligent.com/resources/blog/diversity); it’s not only essential to the “S” in ESG, but it’s also the best way to tap into the many experiences and backgrounds it takes to address ESG effectively.

### 2. Choose an ESG framework

Thousands of ESG regulations are on the books, but not all may be relevant to your specific ESG reporting needs. Choosing the right framework remains an ESG best practice because it’s essential to setting your program up for success.

Organizations that create a framework themselves can too often misconstrue their ESG performance by simply disclosing how effective they are at the priorities they’ve identified on their own. Adhering to an accepted framework, however, shows that you’re committed to a more holistic view of ESG performance — one that’s tied as much to the world around you as it is to your objectives.

Some of the most common ESG reporting frameworks include:

### 3. Set ESG goals

It’s critical that you set [ESG goals](https://www.diligent.com/resources/blog/esg-goals) to serve as your program’s north star. These goals should be related to the framework you selected. If you selected [GRI](https://www.diligent.com/resources/blog/gri-standards), for example, you might prioritize areas the framework already emphasizes: your [GHG emissions](https://www.diligent.com/insights/esg/how-to-measure-greenhouse-gas-emissions/), waste attributed to your production, how safe your facilities are, etc.

This ESG best practice will help you make intentional, strategic changes that will reduce your impact on the environment and your community. It also makes it easier to disclose the results of your ESG efforts.

### 4. Identify metrics for your goals

Goals alone aren’t enough. That’s because your ESG goals are broad statements about what you hope to achieve. Metrics, on the other hand, are the specific data points you’ll use to measure your progress. For example, if pay equity is your goal a key metric could be CEO pay ratios.

As you research and select data points, [metrics](https://www.diligent.com/resources/blog/metrics) that meet our ESG best practices are those that are:

### 5. Collect ESG data

[ESG data](https://www.diligent.com/resources/blog/esg-data-management) is central to your efforts. Many corporations engage in what’s called greenwashing, which is an attempt to appear committed to ESG without taking any action. Corporations with clear reporting and data can easily prove that their efforts are genuine and contribute to meaningful, measurable impact.

It’s an ESG best practice to not only collect and centralize data related to your ESG metrics but to translate that information into an [ESG score](https://www.diligent.com/resources/blog/esg-risk-scores). Your ESG score will indicate your risks and opportunities, the efficacy of your initiatives and how effectively the board is managing ESG.

### 6. Strategize on improvements

In ESG reporting, metrics should be actionable. If the metrics show that you have yet to meet your ESG goals, consider how you can adapt your strategy to make more progress. If you have met your goals, consider redefining that goal or setting a new one, pivoting to ESG areas you have yet to emphasize.

It’s important to focus on improvements that will meaningfully impact your business. If, for example, you ship products overseas, the ESG best practice is to create a strategy that reduces *those* emissions — not just the electricity usage at your company’s headquarters.

### 7. Create visibility around ESG performance

Investors, regulators and the board want visibility into your ESG program. For this reason, ESG reporting isn’t only about creating internal benchmarks. It’s translating those benchmarks into clear, centralized reports that prove your commitment to investors, prove your compliance to regulators and prove the value of ESG to your board.

ESG reporting best practices state that you have a single source of truth for all reporting, that your reports offer real-time insights and that dashboards are clear and easy to interpret.

### 8. Monitor ESG

ESG issues rapidly evolve. [Policy](https://www.diligent.com/resources/blog/policy) and industry leaders update frameworks and expectations. New ESG priorities take hold among investors. The board may ask for visibility into ESG areas they consider important for the corporation’s future.

Each of these scenarios, and more, can impact the success of your ESG program. It’s also possible that your [ESG strategy](https://www.diligent.com/resources/blog/esg-strategy) won’t perform as expected. In either case, monitoring is how you can identify problems before they develop into larger threats. It’s a best practice to consider ESG an always-on function and closely monitor regulatory, political and shareholder updates and your own ESG data to ensure your efforts don’t fall behind.

### 9. Utilize ESG technology

ESG may be motivated by a desire to protect people and the planet, but in reality, it’s heavily influenced by data. From emissions to waste, biodiversity to human rights, and so much more, corporations are expected to track and centralize numerous metrics and then use those metrics to prove their continued commitment to sustainability.

Manually setting goals, tracking metrics and reporting on ESG is time-consuming. ESG technology can automate much of that process by defining key indicators, reporting on those indicators, and then centralizing your data into a single source of truth. This is the best way to create an ESG program that’s actionable for the board and defensible to investors and regulators.

## Lead your industry on ESG

Modern ESG is still a relatively new function, which means there’s an opportunity to lead the way on ESG best practices. Using the right solutions can signal to investors and regulators that you take ESG seriously. Those same solutions can also equip your board with the clear and credible insights they need to make informed decisions about ESG.

Diligent ESG helps generate accurate sustainability reports by automating data collection and creating thorough, audit-ready reports. See how [Diligent ESG](https://www.diligent.com/solutions/carbon-accounting) can be the single source of truth your organization needs to chart a path forward on ESG.

This article will cover best practices in ESG reporting, focusing on how businesses can enhance their corporate responsibility, improve decision-making, and drive sustainability outcomes. By providing transparency on a company’s environmental impact, social initiatives, and governance practices, ESG reporting builds trust with stakeholders and shows that the company is accountable for its actions. **_Sustainability Accounting Standards Board (SASB)_**Provides industry-specific standards for reporting financially material ESG topics, helping link ESG efforts to financial performance.Manufacturing companies may report on water usage, while tech companies report on data privacy and energy consumption.

Best practices in ESG reporting: enhancing corporate responsibility
===================================================================

12 minute read ⏱

 Last update: May 2025

Environmental, Social, and Governance (ESG) reporting is a cornerstone of modern corporate responsibility. As sustainability and ethical governance become increasingly critical for businesses, ESG reporting helps organizations showcase their commitment to addressing societal challenges, reducing environmental impact, and ensuring strong governance practices.

More than just a compliance exercise, effective ESG reporting enables companies to build stronger relationships with stakeholders, enhance transparency, and contribute to long-term financial performance.

With mounting pressure from regulatory frameworks like the Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive (CSRD) and the increasing expectations of investors and consumers, companies must integrate ESG reporting into their core business strategy.

This article will cover best practices in ESG reporting, focusing on how businesses can enhance their corporate responsibility, improve decision-making, and drive sustainability outcomes.

**************The importance of ESG reporting for corporate responsibility**************
----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

[ESG](https://www.imd.org/blog/governance/esg-policies/) reporting has become a vital tool for businesses to demonstrate their commitment to corporate responsibility and long-term sustainability. By providing transparency on a company’s environmental impact, social initiatives, and governance practices, ESG reporting builds trust with stakeholders and shows that the company is accountable for its actions.

By consistently communicating **ESG performance**, companies can enhance their reputation as ethical corporate citizens. Here’s why ESG reporting is so important to organizations in current times:

### **Improves transparency and trust**

Transparent ESG reporting allows companies to openly communicate their impact on key areas such as climate change, human rights, and [corporate governance](https://www.imd.org/blog/governance/what-is-corporate-governance/?ref=blog). This transparency strengthens stakeholder trust, which is essential for maintaining a positive reputation in the market.

Companies like Patagonia, for example, have built their brand on transparent ESG reporting, earning the loyalty of environmentally conscious consumers.

### **Addresses stakeholder concerns**

Investors, customers, employees, and communities increasingly expect companies to demonstrate their commitment to sustainability. ESG reporting directly addresses these concerns and shows how the business is responding to issues such as labor practices, [carbon emissions](https://www.imd.org/blog/sustainability/carbon-management/?ref=blog), and corporate governance.

### **Enhances social responsibility**

Social responsibility initiatives are a key component of ESG reporting. Companies can highlight their efforts to improve labor conditions, support [diversity and inclusion](https://www.imd.org/blog/management/diversity-in-the-workplace/?ref=blog), and engage with local communities. Starbucks, for example, is known for its robust reporting on social impact, including its ethical sourcing practices and diversity programs.

**************Common ESG reporting frameworks and standards**************
-------------------------------------------------------------------------

**FRAMEWORK****DESCRIPTION****EXAMPLES OF USAGE**
**_Global Reporting Initiative (GRI)_**A widely used global framework focusing on sustainability reporting, addressing climate change, human rights, and labor practices. Suitable for large multinational corporations.Used by companies like Unilever for comprehensive sustainability reporting.
**_Sustainability Accounting Standards Board (SASB)_**Provides industry-specific standards for reporting financially material ESG topics, helping link ESG efforts to financial performance.Manufacturing companies may report on water usage, while tech companies report on data privacy and energy consumption.
**_Task Force on Climate-related Financial Disclosures (TCFD)_**Focuses on climate-related risks and opportunities, integrating climate issues into financial reporting. Crucial for high-emission industries.Shell uses it to report on risks related to the energy transition.
**_Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive (CSRD)_**European regulatory framework mandating large companies to disclose detailed sustainability information, impacting companies operating in Europe.Relevant for European companies that need to comply with sustainability disclosures.

Selecting the right ESG reporting framework is crucial for companies aiming to meet both regulatory requirements and stakeholder expectations. Several established frameworks guide businesses in measuring and reporting on their ESG performance, helping them ensure consistency, transparency, and comparability across industries.

Popular ESG reporting frameworks and standards include:

### **Global Reporting Initiative (GRI)**

One of the most widely used frameworks globally, GRI focuses on sustainability reporting and addresses topics such as climate change, human rights, and labor practices. The GRI standards are designed to be comprehensive and applicable across sectors, making them suitable for large multinational corporations like Unilever.

### **Sustainability Accounting Standards Board (SASB)**

SASB is particularly useful for companies looking to report on financially material ESG topics. It provides industry-specific standards that help businesses link their ESG efforts directly to their financial performance. For example, SASB standards may guide a manufacturing company in reporting on water usage and pollution control, while a tech company might focus on data privacy and energy consumption.

### **Task Force on Climate-related Financial Disclosures (TCFD)**

Focused on climate-related risks and opportunities, TCFD helps companies integrate climate-related issues into their financial reporting. It’s particularly important for organizations in high-emission industries like energy and transport, where climate risks can have a significant financial impact.

For instance, Shell uses TCFD guidelines to report on how it is managing risks related to the global energy transition.

### **Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive (CSRD)**

As the new regulatory framework for European companies, [CSRD](https://www.imd.org/blog/governance/csrd/?ref=blog) mandates that large companies disclose more detailed information on sustainability, including their impact on the environment, social issues, and governance. This directive is set to affect thousands of companies, making it crucial for businesses operating in Europe to comply.

**Key ESG metrics and performance indicators**
----------------------------------------------

Identifying and tracking key ESG metrics is essential for companies looking to improve their sustainability performance and demonstrate accountability. Accurate data collection and analysis are critical to tracking these metrics effectively. Companies must ensure their ESG data is reliable and reflects real-world outcomes.

The following **performance indicators** help businesses measure progress in areas such as environmental impact, social equity, and governance practices.

### **Environmental metrics**

These performance indicators include:

*   Carbon emissions
*   Water usage
*   Waste management

Reducing carbon footprints and transitioning to renewable energy are key goals for many organizations. IKEA, for instance, tracks its carbon emissions to align with its goal of becoming carbon positive by 2030.

### **Social metrics**

These are indicators related to:

*   Labor practices
*   Diversity, equity, and inclusion (DEI)
*   Human rights

Companies should track progress in areas such as employee well-being, gender diversity, and supplier standards. Nike reports extensively on its labor practices within global supply chains, highlighting how it addresses worker safety and ethical sourcing.

### **Governance metrics**

Governance indicators focus on:

*   Board diversity
*   Executive compensation
*   Shareholder rights
*   Anti-corruption efforts

Governance metrics often involve linking executive compensation to ESG performance, ensuring that leadership is accountable for achieving sustainability goals. Microsoft, for instance, has tied executive bonuses to their sustainability objectives, reinforcing the importance of aligning leadership with long-term ESG performance.

**Best practices to strengthen ESG reporting for long-term success**
--------------------------------------------------------------------

Effective ESG reporting is about more than just meeting compliance requirements – it’s a powerful tool for identifying risks, improving decision-making, and aligning sustainability with your overall business strategy.

From managing key ESG risks to engaging stakeholders and reporting on climate change, companies need a clear approach to build trust and ensure transparency.

The following sections will guide you through essential best practices to enhance your ESG reporting, manage risks, align with business goals, and meet regulatory requirements. Whether it’s using data to inform decisions or effectively communicating sustainability efforts, these strategies will help you create a robust, impactful ESG reporting process.

### **1. Identify and manage key ESG risks**

**Materiality assessments** are essential for identifying the ESG issues that matter most to your business and stakeholders. Focusing on the most relevant risks helps improve the quality of your ESG reporting and strengthens your sustainability strategy.

Here’s how materiality assessments can help you manage key risks:

*   **Climate-related risks.**Many industries face significant risks due to climate change, such as extreme weather, regulatory changes, or shifting consumer demand. Using frameworks like TCFD, businesses can identify and report on these risks to mitigate their impact.
*   **Supply chain****risks.**Companies must assess risks like human rights violations, environmental damage, and legal compliance in their supply chains. Proactively managing these risks improves transparency.
*   **Governance risks.**Weak governance can lead to regulatory fines and reputational damage. Regularly conducting materiality assessments helps companies prioritize governance issues that affect both stakeholders and performance.

Materiality assessments help ensure that your ESG reporting is focused, relevant, and impactful by identifying and managing these critical risks.

### **2. Improve data collection**

Accurate ESG data collection is crucial for transparent and reliable ESG reporting. Without the right data, companies can’t effectively track their ESG performance or make informed decisions about sustainability efforts. Here are some strategies to enhance your data collection process:

*   **Leverage technology for data management.**Automating the ESG data collection process with specialized software can streamline reporting and ensure consistency. Platforms like Enablon and Datamaran help businesses collect, analyze, and report ESG data in real time, making the process more efficient.
*   **Utilize established****methodologies****.** Applying recognized methods like lifecycle assessments and carbon accounting standards ensures that your data accurately reflects your environmental and social impact. These methodologies add credibility to your ESG reports by providing robust and meaningful insights.
*   **Partner with external****providers****.**Collaborating with third-party ESG data providers can help verify the accuracy of your reports and ensure compliance with regulatory requirements. This approach allows businesses to improve decision-making and meet stakeholder expectations with reliable, verified data.

By following these practices, companies can develop a strong ESG reporting process that informs strategic decisions and drives continuous improvement.

### **3. Align ESG reporting with business strategy**

To maximize the effectiveness of ESG reporting, it must be tightly integrated with your company’s overall [business strategy](https://www.imd.org/blog/strategy/business-strategy/). When ESG goals align with core business objectives, companies can drive value creation, enhance decision-making, and achieve sustainable long-term growth.

**Here’s how to align ESG reporting with business strategy:**

*   **Incorporate ESG into****decision-making****.**Aligning ESG reporting with your business strategy allows for more informed decision-making that considers both sustainability risks and opportunities. This helps companies stay ahead of market shifts, meet regulatory demands, and remain competitive. For example, businesses that factor climate risks into their strategic planning can better mitigate financial impacts and seize growth opportunities.
*   **Tie****executive compensation****to****ESG performance****.** Follow the example of [Unilever](https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2014-03-07/unilever-hands-ceo-polman-722-000-bonus-for-sustainability-work) – a company that increasingly links executive compensation to its ESG metrics. This ensures that leadership is accountable for driving sustainability efforts, making ESG a measurable, incentivized priority across the organization.
*   **Strengthen governance structures.**By aligning ESG reporting with corporate governance, businesses can ensure ESG considerations are embedded into operational and strategic decisions. Companies with robust governance structures are better positioned to manage stakeholder expectations and navigate complex regulatory requirements.

When ESG reporting is integrated into the overall business strategy, companies can create a growth roadmap that balances profitability with sustainability, ensuring both financial success and long-term resilience.

### **4. Engage stakeholders**

Engaging stakeholders in the ESG reporting process is essential for building trust and aligning your sustainability efforts with their expectations.

Here’s how to engage stakeholders effectively:

*   **Proactive communication.**Engage stakeholders early in the reporting process to identify their key concerns and expectations. This ensures your ESG reporting addresses the most relevant issues for both the company and its stakeholders.
*   **Transparent in reporting.**Keep stakeholders informed with regular updates on ESG performance. Transparent communication builds trust with shareholders, investors, and communities. For example, Danone has successfully maintained strong relationships by providing frequent updates on its sustainability efforts.

Proactively engaging stakeholders can help you create an ESG strategy that reflects their concerns and maintains transparency and accountability.

### **5. Include ESG initiatives and sustainability efforts**

Transparent reporting on ESG initiatives is critical to demonstrating the company’s progress toward sustainability goals. Whether it’s reducing carbon emissions, improving labor conditions, or enhancing community engagement, businesses need to effectively communicate their efforts to stakeholders.

Best practices for reporting ESG initiatives and sustainability efforts include:

*   **Being transparent about challenges and successes.**Companies should share both their achievements and the challenges they face in meeting their sustainability goals. For instance, IKEA regularly reports on its progress toward becoming a circular business but also highlights the difficulties in reaching this goal.
*   **Providing real-world examples.**Case studies and real-world examples of ESG initiatives in action help stakeholders understand the tangible impact of the company’s efforts. For example, Adidas frequently highlights its use of recycled material

![Image 17: ESG Strategy](https://aplanet.org/resources/wp-content/uploads/2024/02/ESG-Strategy.png)

**ESG strategy (Environmental, Social, and Governance)**is an integral focal point that companies adopt to operate sustainably and ethically.

Here you will find a complete guide to develop your company’s ESG strategy. We’ll do an exhaustive analysis of a company’s operations to identify areas for improvement and risks associated with sustainability.

Furthermore, we’ll look into establishing objectives, designing politics and procedures, and finally, the implementation of tracking and reporting mechanisms to evaluate progress.

Introduction to ESG Strategies
------------------------------

In the age of impact and corporate sustainability, focusing on ESG strategies is vital for any organisation that looks to flourish.

Practices centred on environmental, social and governance corporate criteria are catalysts for innovation and resilience in any company.

What is an ESG strategy?
------------------------

An ESG strategy is an integral**focus for a company to incorporate environmental, social and governance practices in their business model.**

Implementing an effective ESG strategy improves corporate reputation and also brings with it long term economic benefits, including client and employee loyalty alongside better market positioning.

In an environment where investors and regulators value sustainability more and more, ESG strategies have become fundamental for company success.

[![Image 18: CSRD Guide_EN](https://no-cache.hubspot.com/cta/default/5618409/interactive-147936357002.png)](https://cta-service-cms2.hubspot.com/web-interactives/public/v1/track/redirect?encryptedPayload=AVxigLJFUGrkXBmyHL4HDGwXC5Euupvkero8Bj2vRP3MOFzmstGwfh6N1F%2FUgeJk7MR0VvkAvmeofeA749Kh8ZX9xPRe3RGURKnIf%2FnCqgYfZVLfmu9hOWYn%2FOODf87GwSjgSGwFbVfK8Lx1tT%2FEDBH1DddppgP33HWBcfnYCAiqT3sJnYfl1iDv%2Fuvyu9gDeg%3D%3D&webInteractiveContentId=147936357002&portalId=5618409&__hstc=115348697.267c8f50cb5cc43a6a8c21ad0a4014fc.1748322152683.1748322152683.1748322152683.1&__hssc=115348697.1.1748322152683&__hsfp=1615362440)

### The importance of ESG strategy in the Corporate Landscape

ESG strategy is a compass **guiding companies** towards ethically generated economic prosperity. Innovation, social responsibility and environmental leadership form this strategic tripod.

Here you can see some of the advantages for any given company on developing an ESG strategy:

1.   Reduction of financial risks and improved reputation.
2.   Increase in the capture of conscious investors.
3.   Movement towards a resilient and adaptable organisational culture.
4.   Attraction and retention of high quality talent, perceived as a company committed to sustainable and ethical values.
5.   Stimulating operational innovation and efficiency, finding more sustainable and efficient solutions.

The integration of these principles is vital for the business fabric of the future.

SUMMARY:
While ESG reporting presents significant opportunities for businesses—such as enhancing brand reputation, improving stakeholder trust, and attracting sustainable investments—it also comes with complex challenges that companies must overcome to ensure accuracy, compliance, and strategic alignment. This absence of a universal ESG reporting framework creates confusion for companies trying to align their sustainability disclosures with multiple reporting guidelines and regulations. As Environmental, Social, and Governance (ESG) reporting becomes a key requirement for businesses, regulatory bodies across the globe are tightening ESG disclosure rules to ensure transparency, accountability, and risk management. Unlike voluntary sustainability reporting in the past, ESG regulations are now mandatory in many regions, compelling businesses to disclose climate risks, social impact, and corporate governance practices in financial filings.

The 5 Main Challenges of ESG Reporting and Best Practices
=========================================================

February 25, 2025

[](https://ecoactivetech.com/the-5-main-challenges-of-esg-reporting-and-best-practices/#)

Environmental, Social, and Governance (ESG) reporting has become a critical aspect of corporate transparency, enabling businesses to showcase their commitment to sustainability, social responsibility, and ethical governance. With investors, regulators, and consumers demanding greater transparency, organizations are under increasing pressure to integrate ESG considerations into their corporate strategies and financial disclosures.

While ESG reporting presents significant opportunities for businesses—such as enhancing brand reputation, improving stakeholder trust, and attracting sustainable investments—it also comes with complex challenges that companies must overcome to ensure accuracy, compliance, and strategic alignment.

From navigating multiple ESG frameworks to managing vast amounts of ESG data, organizations must adopt structured approaches and leverage advanced ESG reporting platforms like EcoActive to streamline processes and maintain compliance with evolving global standards.

**Challenge 1: Navigating Multiple ESG Reporting Frameworks**

**The Problem: No Universal Standard for ESG Reporting**

ESG (Environmental, Social, and Governance) reporting has become an essential part of corporate disclosure, with investors, regulators, and consumers demanding greater transparency in sustainability efforts. However, unlike financial reporting—which follows standardized frameworks like Generally Accepted Accounting Principles (GAAP) or International Financial Reporting Standards (IFRS)—ESG reporting lacks a single global standard.

This absence of a universal ESG reporting framework creates confusion for companies trying to align their sustainability disclosures with multiple reporting guidelines and regulations. Businesses, especially multinational corporations (MNCs), must comply with various ESG reporting frameworks depending on their industry, region, and investor requirements.

**Why Does the Lack of a Universal ESG Standard Matter?**

**Regulatory Complexity: Different countries and governing bodies impose varying ESG disclosure rules, making compliance challenging and resource-intensive.**

**Inconsistent ESG Data Collection: Companies may collect and report sustainability data using different methodologies, leading to discrepancies in ESG performance evaluations.**

**Stakeholder Confusion: Investors, customers, and regulators struggle to compare ESG reports across companies when multiple frameworks use different metrics and reporting standards.**

**Risk of Greenwashing: Without a single standard, some companies exaggerate or misrepresent their sustainability efforts, leading to reputational risks and potential regulatory penalties.**

**Overview of Key ESG Reporting Frameworks**

Organizations looking to develop an effective ESG reporting strategy must navigate multiple frameworks that cater to different aspects of sustainability disclosures. Below are the most widely recognized ESG reporting frameworks used globally:

1. **Global Reporting Initiative (GRI)**

**Best for: Companies seeking broad sustainability reporting across industries.**

GRI is the most widely adopted ESG reporting standard, designed to help businesses communicate their sustainability impacts on the economy, environment, and society.

Provides comprehensive ESG disclosure guidelines covering carbon emissions, labor rights, anti-corruption measures, and more.

Used by over 10,000 organizations worldwide, including Fortune 500 companies and government agencies.

2. **Sustainability Accounting Standards Board (SASB)**

**Best for: Investors and businesses looking for industry-specific ESG performance metrics.**

SASB focuses on financial materiality by identifying ESG factors that impact a company’s financial performance.

 Industry-specific standards cover 77 industries, making it a preferred framework for investors assessing ESG risks and opportunities.

3. **Task Force on Climate-related Financial Disclosures (TCFD)**

**Best for: Companies required to disclose climate-related financial risks.**

Established by the Financial Stability Board (FSB), TCFD focuses on climate risk disclosure to help businesses and investors understand the financial impact of climate change.

Increasingly becoming mandatory in the U.K., EU, and parts of Asia.

4. **International Sustainability Standards Board (ISSB)**

**Best for: Companies looking for a unified global ESG reporting standard.**

Launched by the International Financial Reporting Standards (IFRS) Foundation, ISSB aims to standardize ESG reporting globally.

Expected to harmonize ESG disclosures by integrating elements from GRI, SASB, and TCFD into a single reporting framework.

5. **EU Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive (CSRD)**

**Best for: Businesses operating in Europe or with European investors.**

The CSRD replaces the Non-Financial Reporting Directive (NFRD) and expands ESG disclosure requirements for companies operating in the European Union (EU).

Introduces the concept of double materiality, meaning companies must report both financial risks and broader social/environmental impacts.

Over 50,000 companies will be required to comply, making it one of the most influential ESG regulations worldwide.

 6. **SEC Climate Disclosure Rule (U.S.)**

**Best for: Publicly traded companies in the United States.**

The U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) is introducing mandatory climate risk disclosures, requiring companies to report greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions, climate-related risks, and financial impacts.

Aims to increase transparency and protect investors from ESG-related financial risks.

7. **CDP (Carbon Disclosure Project)**

CDP is a leading global environmental disclosure system that helps companies, cities, states, and regions measure and manage their impact on climate, water, and forests.

**What it does:**

Collects standardized climate, water, and forest-related data from companies and governments.

Provides insights into corporate sustainability efforts and their alignment with global climate goals.

Used by investors, policymakers, and businesses to assess environmental risks and opportunities.

**Why it matters:**

Over 24,800+ companies and 1,000+ cities report through CDP, covering 66% of global market capitalization.

 Helps organizations identify climate risks, track emissions, and improve sustainability performance.

 Supports compliance with TCFD, CSRD, and other regulatory frameworks.

**8. Taskforce on Nature-related Financial Disclosures (TNFD)**

TNFD is a nature-focused ESG framework designed to help companies identify and report risks related to biodiversity and ecosystems—just as TCFD does for climate risks.

**What it does:**

Provides a structured approach to assessing, managing, and disclosing nature-related financial risks.

 Encourages companies to consider deforestation, biodiversity loss, and ecosystem degradation in financial decision-making.

Aligns with TCFD, ensuring consistency in climate and nature-based reporting.

**Why it matters:**

Helps businesses shift financial flows away from activities that harm biodiversity and toward nature-positive investments.

Supports the Global Biodiversity Framework (GBF) and global sustainability goals.

Strengthens risk assessment for industries dependent on natural resources (e.g., agriculture, energy, mining, and finance).

9. **UN Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs)**

The 17 SDGs are a global blueprint for economic, environmental, and social sustainability, adopted by the United Nations (UN) in 2015 to be achieved by 2030.

**What it does:**

Defines clear sustainability targets across climate action, responsible consumption, gender equality, and more.

 Provides a common global framework for governments, businesses, and civil society to align sustainability initiatives.

Helps companies integrate ESG strategies with broader global sustainability goals.

**Why it matters:**

Used as a benchmark for ESG impact measurement and reporting.

Encourages businesses to align operations, supply chains, and investments with sustainable practices.

Investors and stakeholders increasingly assess corporate SDG alignment when making decisions.

**Best Practices: How to Navigate Multiple ESG Frameworks**

**Identify the Most Relevant ESG Standards**

Businesses should analyze their industry, geographic presence, and stakeholder expectations to determine which ESG frameworks best align with their reporting needs.

For example, a financial institution may prioritize SASB, while a manufacturing company may focus on TCFD’s climate-related disclosures.

**Conduct a Gap Analysis**

Perform an internal assessment to compare current ESG reporting efforts against the requirements of relevant frameworks.

Identify areas needing improvement to enhance compliance and reporting quality.

**Utilize ESG Reporting Technology**

EcoActive, an advanced ESG reporting platform, enables organizations to align with multiple ESG frameworks simultaneously by automating data collection, reporting, and compliance tracking.

**Stay Updated on Regulatory Changes**

Governments and regulators frequently update ESG disclosure requirements. Companies must monitor these changes through sources like:

SEC’s ESG reporting regulations (U.S.)

EU’s CSRD and Sustainable Finance Disclosure Regulation (SFDR)

UK’s FCA TCFD-aligned ESG guidelines

**Challenge 2: Understanding and Complying with ESG Regulations**

**The Problem: Constantly Changing ESG Regulatory Landscape**

As Environmental, Social, and Governance (ESG) reporting becomes a key requirement for businesses, regulatory bodies across the globe are tightening ESG disclosure rules to ensure transparency, accountability, and risk management.

For companies, this rapidly evolving regulatory landscape creates significant compliance challenges, requiring them to adapt quickly to new mandates, avoid non-compliance penalties, and maintain stakeholder trust.

Unlike voluntary sustainability reporting in the past, ESG regulations are now mandatory in many regions, compelling businesses to disclose climate risks, social impact, and corporate governance practices in financial filings.

**Why Is ESG Compliance a Challenge for Businesses?**

Constantly Evolving Regulations – ESG laws are frequently updated, requiring companies to track and adapt to new compliance requirements.

Complex ESG Disclosure Requirements – Different countries enforce varied reporting standards, making compliance difficult for multinational corporations (MNCs).

High Cost of ESG Compliance – Businesses must invest in ESG reporting software, regulatory consultants, and internal compliance teams to manage evolving rules.

Risk of Non-Compliance Penalties – Failure to meet ESG disclosure obligations can result in fines, legal actions, reputational damage, and investor withdrawals.

Lack of Standardized Global ESG Regulations – While financial reporting follows GAAP or IFRS, ESG reporting lacks one unified global standard, creating compliance confusion.

**Key ESG Regulatory Developments Worldwide**

Several major regulatory changes have been introduced to enforce ESG reporting compliance. Below are some of the most significant:

1. **SEC’s Climate Disclosure Rule (U.S.) – Effective 2025**

The U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) has introduced a climate-related financial disclosure rule requiring publicly traded companies to report:

Greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions (Scope 1, Scope 2, and in some cases, Scope 3 emissions).

Climate-related financial risks and their impact on business operations.

Board oversight and governance of climate risks.

Failure to comply could result in fines, investor lawsuits, and SEC penalties.

2. **EU’s Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive (CSRD) – Effective 2024**

Expands ESG reporting requirements for large companies operating in the European Union.

Introduces double materiality, meaning companies must disclose both:

How ESG risks impact their financial performance.

How their operations affect the environment and society.

Over 50,000 companies must comply, making CSRD one of the most comprehensive ESG regulations worldwide.

3. **UK’s TCFD-aligned ESG Disclosure Rules – Mandatory for Large Businesses**

The Task Force on Climate-related Financial Disclosures (TCFD) framework is now mandatory for:

Premium-listed companies on the London Stock Exchange.

Banks, insurers, and large private companies.

Companies must disclose climate risks, transition plans, and financial impacts of environmental issues.

4. **ISSB’s Global Sustainability Disclosure Standards (2024)**

The International Sustainability Standards Board (ISSB), created by the IFRS Foundation, aims to establish a global ESG reporting baseline.

Integrates elements from GRI, SASB, and TCFD to create one unified sustainability disclosure standard.

Expected to harmonize ESG reporting across multiple regions.

**The Impact of ESG Regulations on Businesses**

**For Publicly Traded Companies: Must comply with SEC, CSRD, or ISSB disclosure rules to maintain investor confidence and prevent stock price drops due to ESG non-compliance.**

**For Private Companies: Even non-public businesses are now being asked by investors and supply chain partners to disclose ESG risks to maintain business relationships.**

**For Multinational Corporations: Companies with global operations must align with multiple ESG laws, requiring extensive compliance tracking.**

**For SMEs (Small and Medium Enterprises): While direct ESG mandates may not apply, large corporations require ESG compliance from suppliers, affecting business opportunities.**

**Best Practices: How to Ensure ESG Compliance**

To successfully navigate the complex ESG regulatory landscape, businesses must adopt a structured approach to compliance.

**1. Develop an ESG Compliance Team**

Assign dedicated ESG professionals to monitor regulatory updates and reporting obligations.

Engage legal experts and sustainability officers to ensure full compliance.

Conduct regular internal audits to assess ESG reporting accuracy.

**2. Leverage ESG Compliance Technology**

Use ESG reporting platforms like EcoActive to:

Track evolving ESG regulations across different jurisdictions.

Automate compliance reporting to reduce manual workload.

Integrate ESG data from different departments into one standardized report.

**3. Engage with Regulatory Bodies**

Proactively communicate with SEC, EU regulators, and ESG standard-setting organizations.

Participate in public consultations and industry discussions to stay ahead of policy changes.

**4. Provide ESG Compliance Training**

Train internal teams on new ESG disclosure requirements.

Educate executives, board members, and finance teams on the business impact of ESG compliance.

Conduct quarterly ESG workshops to keep employees updated on regulatory changes.

**5. Integrate ESG Compliance into Business Strategy**

Align sustainability goals with corporate risk management and financial reporting.

Use Key Performance Indicators (KPIs) to measure ESG compliance progress.

Ensure board-level oversight on ESG strategy and regulatory obligations.

**The Cost of ESG Non-Compliance**

Failing to comply with ESG regulations can result in serious legal and financial consequences, including:

**Regulatory Fines & Penalties – The SEC, EU regulators, and UK authorities impose heavy fines for non-compliance.*
